answer: Argon2id with parameters: 64MB memory, 3 iterations, 4 parallelism factor
Refresh token expiration: 7 days with rotate-on-use policy (new token issued with each refresh request)
Notification Service runs on port 9200
Currently running PostgreSQL 15.4, upgraded from version 14.9
API Gateway throttle limits: 5000 requests/second burst, 2000 requests/second steady state
Argon2id was chosen over bcrypt for side-channel resistance, providing stronger protection against timing attacks and differential power analysis
Drizzle was chosen over Prisma because it has zero runtime dependencies and provides SQL-like TypeScript syntax, reducing overhead and complexity

Portfolio and Notification services verify auth tokens the same way as all services: the API Gateway Lambda authorizer validates the JWT via the JWKS endpoint and injects verified claims into the request context
Rate limiting is done at the ALB layer instead of in application code to apply DDoS protection before requests reach containers, reducing wasted compute resources

The chain involves: Client requests rebalance to Portfolio Service → Gateway Lambda authorizer validates JWT via JWKS and injects claims → Portfolio Service reads RBAC role from injected claims → role hierarchy middleware enforces authorization before rebalance operation executes

If JWKS endpoint goes down: all downstream auth validation fails and requests are rejected. All services using Lambda authorizer are affected. Grace period: LRU cache with 1000 entry capacity and 5 minute TTL allows some requests through until cache expires
Real-time portfolio alerting depends on both the Notification Service and a WebSocket API in the Gateway. Status: planned for Q3, not yet implemented
Yes, the UTC+14 token expiry bug has been fixed. Solution: use Unix timestamps instead of Date objects to prevent timezone-based expiry calculation errors

Implement webhook notifications for auth events: define new event types in notification schema (e.g., auth.login_success), register webhooks via POST /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register with expiration, emit events from Auth Service, use exponential backoff retry policy (5 attempts/24h), include traceId in RFC7807 Problem Details for debugging
Maximum simultaneous database connections: 5 Auth Service instances × 20 connections per instance = 100 connections

Connection pool interaction: 5 instances share a pool of 20 connections each. During large tax-lot processing via Celery, unbounded memory growth in Celery workers can exhaust available connections if workers don't release them, requiring investigation and fixes to Celery memory management

No, Fargate Spot cannot be used for Auth Service. Spot instances can be interrupted and Auth Service must be always-on. Spot is ONLY for batch workloads (portfolio-recalc, digest) where interruption risk is acceptable

The doctrine prohibiting in-memory session cache: the JWKS validation pattern already provides efficient caching via LRU with 5-minute TTL. Adding a session cache would duplicate caching logic, increase memory overhead, and complicate invalidation. Stateless design ensures horizontal scalability

30-day backup retention supports RPO/RTO targets: RPO=1 hour (backups allow recovery to 1-hour-old state), RTO=4 hours (30-day retention ensures compliance and provides recovery point availability during incident investigation and execution)
RDS upgrade from 14.9 to 15.4 required connection string change: sslmode changed to verify-full and global-bundle.pem certificate was added for SSL/TLS verification in transit
tax_lots table columns: id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, created_at

MFA affects login flow: after password validation via Argon2id verify, if MFA is enabled, the response prompts for MFA verification. Client must complete MFA (TOTP or recovery code) before access token is issued
10 recovery codes are generated per MFA setup. Each recovery code is single-use and permanently consumed once used, preventing replay attacks
Webhook retry policy: exponential backoff with 5 attempts within 24 hours, plus admin override option to retry or skip failed webhooks

Complete security posture for credential data: At rest: AES-256-GCM encryption per-tenant keys, password hashing with Argon2id(64MB/3iter/4par). In transit: mTLS enforced via Envoy sidecar, PostgreSQL connections use sslmode=verify-full with global-bundle.pem. Secrets rotated every 90 days

Full login request lifecycle with MFA: Client submits credentials → Gateway validates schema → ALB rate limit (1000/min/IP) → Lambda authorizer passes through → Auth Service receives request → Argon2id verify password → MFA enabled check → if enabled, issue temporary MFA challenge token → client completes TOTP or recovery code submission → Auth Service verifies TOTP or consumes recovery code → issue RS256 access token (15min) + refresh token (7day, rotate-on-use) → return tokens to client

For new Billing Service in Rust: apply naming patterns (handleVerb{Resource}), use RFC7807 Problem Details with traceId for errors, implement structured logging with request ID via context, require Claude Code review before merge, auth validation via same JWKS pattern (either Lambda authorizer or internal token validation), use real database testing (testcontainers equivalent for Rust), follow AppMesh/Envoy mTLS for service mesh integration, enforce 90-day secret rotation

1500 req/min from single IP hitting Auth Service: Layer 1 (ALB): VPCLink rate limit 1000/min/IP triggers rejection, excess 500 req/min dropped before containers. Remaining 1000 req/min passed through. Layer 2 (Auth Service): receives ~1000 req/min, processes within capacity. Result: 500 requests rejected at ALB with rate limit response

All system components depending on JWT signing key: Auth Service (issues tokens using RS256 private key), JWKS endpoint (serves public key via /.well-known/jwks.json), API Gateway Lambda authorizer (validates tokens using public key from JWKS), Portfolio Service (validates tokens via same Lambda authorizer), Notification Service (validates tokens via same Lambda authorizer). Private key stored locally in Auth Service, never shared

Complete deployment pipeline: Developer commits to GitHub → GitHub Actions triggers: lint, typecheck, test, build, push image to ECR, deploy to ECS → Claude Code review required before merge to main → blue-green deployment: new task definition targets old ALB target group, then switch traffic → zero-downtime cutover. Fargate Spot used ONLY for batch tasks (portfolio-recalc, digest), never for always-on services
